coredata.dc:title: Highly fluorescent N-doped carbon nanodots as an effective multi-probe quenching system for the determination of nitrite, nitrate and ferric ions in food matrices
coredata.dc:creator: Karali, Katerina K.
Sygellou, Lamprini
Stalikas, Constantine D.
coredata.dc:description:
Tris-(hydroxymethyl)-aminomethane and urea were used as low-cost precursor compounds to synthesize highly fluorescent N-doped carbon nanodots (CNDs), in an environmentally-friendly, inexpensive process. The as-prepared CNDs exhibit blue fluorescence, excellent photostability under various conditions, water dispersibility and stability over several parameters, such as a wide range of pH. The N-doped CNDs were applied as a multi-probe fluorescence quenching system to the sensitive detection of nitrite (
NO
2
−
), nitrate (
NO
3
−
) and ferric (Fe3+) ions in food matrices. The recoveries from spiked food samples were fairly acceptable without significant interferences despite the complexity of the tested matrices. The decrease in fluorescence intensity is in linear relationship with the concentrations of
NO
2
−
,
NO
3
−
and Fe3+, in the ranges of 0.015–1.11 mM, 0.072–0.60 mM and 2.9–176 μΜ, respectively. The as-synthesized carbon dots were used for the detection of
NO
2
−
,
NO
3
−
and Fe3+ in food matrices after proper pretreatment, concluding that the multi-probe fluorescence system may potentially be implemented in food control. The FRET mechanism is able to describe the quenching of the CNDs-
NO
2
−
system, while the proportional temperature-dependent relationship with the slopes of calibration plots hint at a dynamic quenching mechanism. In the case of the CNDs-Fe3+ system, the slopes exhibit an inverse temperature dependence, indicating a static mechanism while there is no indication of a FRET mechanism.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doped carbon nanodots
Nitrite ions
Nitrate ions
Ferric ions
Food matrices
Quenching mechanisms

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Nitrite, nitrate and ferric ions probing
Stability of CNDs under different conditions

Optimization of
NO
2
−
,
NO
3
−
and Fe3+ probing

Fluorescent determination of
NO
2
−
,
NO
3
−
and Fe3+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FT-IR spectra of the dots synthesized by the precursor compounds: Tris (purple curve), urea (grey curve), Tris/urea (green curve).
Deconvoluted (a) C1s, (b) O1s and (c) N1s core level XP spectra.
(A): Excitation-dependent emission spectra of the CNDs, (B): excitation spectrum of CNDs at an emission wavelength of 390 nm.

Selectivity study of the CNDs in optimum conditions for (A)
NO
2
−
and (B) Fe3+ probing.

Plot of fluorescence quenching versus
NO
2
−
concentration. Inset: linear calibration curve in the low-concentration range.

Plot of fluorescence quenching versus
NO
3
−
concentration. Inset: linear calibration curve in the low-concentration range.
Plot of fluorescence quenching versus Fe3+ concentration. Inset: linear calibration curve in the low-concentration range.

Normalized excitation and emission spectra of CNDs and absorption spectrum of
NO
2
−
.

Analysis of food matrices for
NO
2
−
,
NO
3
−
and recoveries at two spiking concentration levels.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The values in brackets represent the concentrations of the anions by applying the reference method.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The spiking was carried out in the final extract after sample treatment and the detected values refer to the concentrations in the final solutions.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The values in brackets represent the concentrations of the analyte by applying the reference method.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The spiking was carried out after acid treatment and the detected values refer to the concentrations in the final solutions.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Binding constants K and binding sites “n” for the studied probing systems.

head.ce:title: Highly fluorescent N-doped carbon nanodots as an effective multi-probe quenching system for the determination of nitrite, nitrate and ferric ions in food matrices

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry, Department of Chemistry, University of Ioannina, 45110 Ioannina, Greece

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry, Department of Chemistry, University of Ioannina

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Foundation for Research and Technology Hellas/Institute of Chemical Engineering Sciences (FORTH/ICE-HT), Stadiou Str., P.O. Box 1414, GR – 26504 Rio-Patras, Greece

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Foundation for Research and Technology Hellas/Institute of Chemical Engineering Sciences (FORTH/ICE-HT)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Tris-(hydroxymethyl)-aminomethane and urea were used as low-cost precursor compounds to synthesize highly fluorescent N-doped carbon nanodots (CNDs), in an environmentally-friendly, inexpensive process. The as-prepared CNDs exhibit blue fluorescence, excellent photostability under various conditions, water dispersibility and stability over several parameters, such as a wide range of pH. The N-doped CNDs were applied as a multi-probe fluorescence quenching system to the sensitive detection of nitrite (
NO
2
−
), nitrate (
NO
3
−
) and ferric (Fe3+) ions in food matrices. The recoveries from spiked food samples were fairly acceptable without significant interferences despite the complexity of the tested matrices. The decrease in fluorescence intensity is in linear relationship with the concentrations of
NO
2
−
,
NO
3
−
and Fe3+, in the ranges of 0.015–1.11 mM, 0.072–0.60 mM and 2.9–176 μΜ, respectively. The as-synthesized carbon dots were used for the detection of
NO
2
−
,
NO
3
−
and Fe3+ in food matrices after proper pretreatment, concluding that the multi-probe fluorescence system may potentially be implemented in food control. The FRET mechanism is able to describe the quenching of the CNDs-
NO
2
−
system, while the proportional temperature-dependent relationship with the slopes of calibration plots hint at a dynamic quenching mechanism. In the case of the CNDs-Fe3+ system, the slopes exhibit an inverse temperature dependence, indicating a static mechanism while there is no indication of a FRET mechanism.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past five years, carbon nanodots (CNDs) have evolved as a new kind of fluorescent nanomaterials, whose size is typically smaller than 10 nm. Many synthetic routes for CNDs have been reported, leading to diverse surface properties, owing to different percentage of surface carboxyl and hydroxyl groups [1,2]. These characteristics may be modified by various synthetic conditions, such as chemical modification and surface passivation with many organic, polymeric, inorganic or biological materials, a process known as doping. Because of these processes, the florescence and physical properties of CNDs are altered to a considerable extent [1]. The diverse characteristics of CNDs make them suitable for different kinds of applications such as analytical methodologies [3–5], chemical sensing and probing [6], electrochemical applications and many others [7]. Moreover, their extremely small size, enables their penetration through cell membrane, lending themselves to being used in biological applications, such as bioimaging [3,5,8], biosensing [9], drug delivery [10] etc.

A developing interest has been observed over the last years for the use of natural sources, like various kinds of food waste, honey or wool [3,5,11,12] to synthesize carbon dots. However, certain drawbacks came into view, as these types of carbon dots potentially exhibit broad-size distribution and lack of size control, small scale synthesis, low chemical stability and may form undesirable byproducts [13,14]. It is known that the properties of carbonaceous materials prepared from natural or residual resources depend on the age, time of collection and the site where the products are grown. This is in contrast to carbon materials made from well-defined chemicals with added sources for N and S or P. These bottom-up methods are characterized as green approaches due to the use of low-cost precursors and the simple steps required, mostly without the need of post-treatment processing [1,15].

Nitrite and nitrate are naturally contained in fruits and, at higher concentrations, in leafy vegetables like spinach and lettuce, which are considered to be a crucial part of a healthy diet. Moreover, they are added as curing agents to food products and processed meats, such as ham and sausages, to provide acceptable color, flavor and antimicrobial properties. Nitrite is, also, a considerably suitable agent to inhibit the growth of the bacteria Clostridium botulinum, whose toxin is fatal [16,17]. Due to their excessive consumption, the presence of these ions has been associated with the formation of the carcinogenic nitrosamines, which are found widely in the natural environment and in several food products, and are relating to several health risks in specific subgroups of populations. For instance, their presence is associated with an increased risk of gastrointestinal cancer and in infants with methemoglobinemia, i.e. the “blue baby syndrome” [16,18]. On the other hand, recent studies, surprisingly, have suggested the consideration of nitrates and nitrites as nutrients, describing their beneficial health effects, such as the vasodilation production, the decrease of blood pressure and the support of the cardiovascular function [17–19]. As a consequence, the systematic monitoring of nitrate and nitrite is considered more than essential and various methods have been reviewed [20]. However novel, sensitive, low-cost and environmentally-friendly methods need to be introduced.

The development of ferric ion detection-systems continues to be extremely substantial, as iron is a highly versatile element for living organisms and contributes to many physiological and biological actions. Deficiency of iron potentially induces anemia while excess of it is a well-known cause of several health implications and illnesses, such as increased risk for cancer, heart diseases, endocrine problems, arthritis, diabetes and liver disease [21]. Therefore, it is important to develop new, effective and reliable methods for ferric ion determination in high-iron content foods, which is generally carried out with AAS or ICP methods [21,22].

The need for new “green” CNDs with propitious and exploitable properties is still growing. To date, there have been few studies in the literature, which use carbon dots for nitrite or/and nitrate probing [23,24]. On the contrary, numerous studies have been reported which use carbon dots in iron detection, in cells and environmental samples [3,25–27]. However, there can scarcely be found one of them which has been applied to food samples. Complex matrices are rarely investigated in association with CNDs as they are rigorous with regard to the development of analytical methods. Herein, a simple and convenient synthesis of N-doped carbon nanodots with high quantum yield, in a cost-effective manner is proposed using environmentally friendly carbon precursor compounds. The determination of nitrite, nitrate and ferric ions in food samples is, then, put forward satisfying the emerging demand for the development of viable fluorescence multi-probe systems. The proposed versatile, fluorescence quenching system shows promising potential as a vanguard of food quality towards safer food consumption [28].
All information about chemicals and materials is provided in the Supplementary material.

Based on the optimum conditions of synthesis, 0.21 g of Tris-(hydroxymethyl)-aminomethane (henceforth called Tris) and 0.13 g of urea (molar ratio of Tris-to-urea = 0.8) were homogenized. The mixture was heated to 210 °C in a stainless steel Teflon-lined autoclave, for 3 h. After treatment, the autoclave was let cool down to room temperature and the generated sticky liquid was collected with double distilled water (DDW). Finally, the solution was freeze-dried and the yellowish product was stored in a dark place for further use. The same procedure using only Tris or urea, was carried out for reasons of comparison.
Details about the instrumentation for probing, characterization and preparation of samples are given in the Supplementary material.
The quantum yield of the synthesized CNDs was determined by a comparative method. Details are given in the Supplementary material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nitrite, nitrate and ferric ions probing
ce:section.ce:para:
For the detection of
NO
3
−
, the species were reduced to
NO
2
−
after 1-min stirring with 60 mg of cadmium powder [29] and the supernatant was handled as described below for
NO
2
−
probing. The detection of
NO
2
−
was achieved as follows: 2.7 mL of a standard solution or a pretreated sample was mixed with 0.3 mL of a CNDs solution of 5 mg/mL. For the detection of Fe3+, 1.7 mL of a standard solution or of a sample was added into 1 mL of Tris/HCl buffer solution (pH 7.0, 0.2 M) and mixed with 0.3 mL of the above CNDs solution. After vortexing for 30 s, the photoluminescence (PL) intensities were recorded at λex/λem = 320/390 nm. The quantification of the analytes in the samples was carried out using calibration curves, which were built by plotting the fluorescence quenching – after monitoring the original (F
0 ) and quenched (F) fluorescence – against quencher concentrations.

The selectivity was examined by measuring the fluorescence quenching percentages caused by heavy metal ions or potential matrix components, such as several anions and amino acids. In other experiments, the fluorescence quenching of
NO
2
−
and Fe3+ were recorded under optimum conditions for each species, in the presence of potential interferences.

All the sample pretreatment procedures were carried out according to the recommendations of the AOAC Official Methods [30], with minor modifications. The determination of nitrite/nitrate took place as shortly as possible after sampling, due to instability of the analytes in extracts. Details about sample pretreatment for nitrite, nitrate and ferric ion determination are given in the Supplementary material.

For the synthesis of CNDs, Tris and urea were chosen as the precursors, as they provide several hydroxyl and amino groups, offering good water solubility and bond-creating ability. The procedure was optimized through a multiparameter optimization procedure including the time and temperature of heating and the molar ratio of the precursors. The optimum conditions were obtained by monitoring the highest fluorescence intensity, which was acquired at a Tris-to-urea ratio of 4:5 (total: 3.9 mmol) and temperature of 210 °C, after heating for 3 h.

The FT-IR spectrum of the as-obtained CNDs (green curve of Fig. 1) exhibits a broad absorption band with two peaks at 3350 and 3230 cm−1 corresponding to the stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H. The absorption band at around 1720 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibrations of C═O [31,32]. This distinctive peak does not appear in the FT-IR spectra of the dots synthesized by the precursors individually. The stretching vibrations of –C═C– (in alkenes) can be seen at 1664 cm−1, while the absorption band at 1585 cm−1 which is assigned to the aromatic stretching vibrations of –C═C and appears in the FT-IR spectra of both Tris- and urea-derived dots, does not become visible in the dots produced from both precursors. In addition, the peaks at 1138 and 1026 cm−1, which are characteristic of the stretching vibrations of the C–N group, suggest that the dots are N-doped [3,5]. The peak at around 1356 cm−1, which is characteristic of symmetric and asymmetric carboxylate stretching (which is present in the urea-derived dots spectrum), as well as that at 3172 cm−1 (shown in Tris-derived dots spectrum) due to the –NH2 group, are absent in the spectrum of the as-synthesized CNDs, indicating the formation of amide bonds. The appearance of the above functional groups can be a crucial factor for the stability and the solubility of the synthesized CNDs.

An XPS analysis was also conducted to further characterize the as-synthesized dots. Fig. 2 shows the deconvoluted XPS C1s (a), O1s (b), and N1s (c) peaks. The C1s peak is analyzed into C–C bonds in sp2 and sp3 configuration at binding energies of 284.6 eV and at 285.5 eV respectively, into C–O bonds at 286.4 eV (epoxides and hyrdoxides), at 288.1 eV (carbonyls and amide groups) and at 289.1 eV (carboxyl groups) [33]. It has to be mentioned that the C1s C–N peak component (C–N bond) appears at ~ 285.5 eV and overlaps with the C–C sp3 bonding configuration. The O1s peak (Fig. 2(b)) is deconvoluted into two components, which are assigned to C–O bonds –C–O(H) or O–C–O at 532.6 eV and C═O bonds C═O(H) or O–C═O(H), at 531.5 eV. Fig. 2(c) shows the N1s peak which is centered at 399.9 ± 0.1 eV and is assigned to amide (O═C–N) or amine/pyrrole groups (C–N–C) [34]. Using the total peak area of O1s, C1s and N1s peaks in each sample, the appropriate sensitivity factors (based on Wagner's collection and adjusted to the transmission characteristics of analyser EA10) and equations, the % average relative atomic content was calculated to be 59.6, 32.6 and 7.8 for C, O and N, respectively.
TEM images of the synthesized CNDs show that the size of the CNDs is 4.5 nm, while their size distribution is narrow (Fig. S1 of Supplementary material).

Aqueous solutions of the synthesized CNDs display light yellow color and bright blue PL, under daylight and UV, respectively (Fig. S2 inset B, A, of Supplementary material). As demonstrated in Fig. S2, the UV–Vis spectrum reveals two characteristic absorption peaks at around 270 and 320 nm, which can be associated with the π-π*transition of conjugated C═C bond and the n-π*transition in the oxygen of C═O bond, respectively [35]. The last one can also be assigned to the transitions of C═C and C–N bonds [5].

The fluorescence spectra of the as-obtained CNDs at excitation wavelengths from 220 to 360 nm, are illustrated in Fig. 3 (A). The optimum emission wavelength for the synthesized CNDs was found to be at 390 nm, after excitation at 320 nm. In addition, the excitation spectrum of Fig. 3 (B) points to a maximum excitation wavelength at 320 nm. The recorded emission spectra of the dots are excitation-independent as changes of the excitation wavelength up to 360nm, cause negligible shift to the maximum emission wavelength. Finally, the quantum yield of the material was calculated to be 55%, using quinine sulfate as reference. It is noteworthy, that this yield is considerably high compared to dots synthesized with similar precursor compounds [36–39]. This elevated value agrees with the reported ones for other materials prepared from Tris, as precursor [25,40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability of CNDs under different conditions
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of aqueous solutions of the as-obtained CNDs was evaluated to examine the influence of various factors on the fluorescence intensity level and to check its applicability to real samples. The PL of the dots was constant in the pH range of 5.2–10.0; beyond this range the PL decreased remarkably (Figs. S3 and S4 of Supplementary material). This is possibly due to the protonation/deprotonation of functional surface groups of the dots, such as hydroxyl-, carboxyl- or amino-groups [41]. In addition, the CNDs were stable in solutions of different ionic strength, at concentrations of NaCl and Na2SO4 up to 1.0 M (Fig. S5 of Supplementary material), implying that the detection system operates properly under these conditions.

Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of solutions prepared by lyophilized CNDs was remarkably stable under storage at room temperature, in a dark place or at 4 °C, for more than two months. Finally, although a temperature from 25 to 45 °C caused a slight reduction of the PL intensity (2% quenching), when a solution of CNDs was irradiated for 1 h with a 150 W Xe lamp, the intensity barely altered, underlining the satisfactory photostability of the as-synthesized material.

ce:section.ce:section-title:
Optimization of
NO
2
−
,
NO
3
−
and Fe3+ probing
ce:section.ce:para:
A preliminary study indicated that the synthesized CNDs could operate as an effective probing system for the determination of
NO
2
−
and Fe3+, as these ions proved to cause fluorescence quenching, in contrast to plenty other anions and heavy metal ions screened at the same concentration. It is known that the complexation with transition metals is supposed to be a potential factor to affect the fluorescence of organic molecules [42]. Noticeably, the dots can be employed to distinguish
NO
2
−
from
NO
3
−
, rendering the material a reasonable multi-probe system for the above anions, as well as for ferric ion.

For the purpose of comparison, the CNDs precursors were treated under the same synthesis conditions. Even though they afforded similar yellowish products with blue PL under UV light, their fluorescence quenching was weak (Tris-derived dots) or absent (urea-derived dots) in the presence of the analytes. Hence, they were not proper to be used as probing systems for the determination of
NO
2
−
,
NO
3
−
and Fe3+. The N-doping provided by urea seems to greatly enhance the detection ability of the resulting carbon- and oxygen-rich nanomaterial.

In order to optimize the conditions for the multi-probe detection, further experiments were conducted, also taking into account the outcome of the stability study. The quenching caused by
NO
2
−
was found to be independent of the pH in the range of 5–10. At pH < 5 and pH > 10 a decrease in the fluorescence quenching was noticed and hence, the detection was performed in neutral pH without the use of buffer solution (Fig. S6 of Supplementary material). As for the detection of Fe3+, a pH study was conducted using a universal-buffer solution (Britton-Robinson) to compensate for variations in the pH due to the fact that the standard working solutions of Fe3+ and the treated samples were prepared in acids. As seen in Fig. S7 of Supplementary material, the Fe3+ PL quenching showed the same trend with that of
NO
2
−
as a function of the pH. Therefore, the procedure was performed in Tris/HCl buffer solution at pH= 7.0 where fluorescence quenching is maximized. The optimum pH was chosen, also, considering the stability of the dots and ensuring that hydrolysis of ferric ion at low working concentrations does not take place [26]. Other factors studied were the ionic strength (NaCl and Na2SO4 up to 1 M), incubation time before measurement, temperature, concentration of the dots and, in case of the
NO
3
−
detection, the quantity of the cadmium powder used for the reduction. The above parameters had an overall little or no effect on the fluorescence quenching. Lastly, Figs. S8 and S9 of Supplementary material show the PL intensity reduction of the CNDs in the presence of
NO
2
−
or Fe3+ at different concentrations, where no shift of the maximum emission is noticed.

ce:section.ce:section-title:
Fluorescent determination of
NO
2
−
,
NO
3
−
and Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para:
The synthesized Tris/urea-derived CNDs were used as a convenient multi-probe fluorescence quenching system for the detection of
NO
2
−
,
NO
3
−
and Fe3+ in food samples. Before confirming their applicability, several selectivity measurements were carried out in optimum conditions for
NO
2
−
and Fe3+ probing ( Fig. 4 (A) and (B), respectively) to confirm the suitability of the system towards target analytes. The selectivity study took place using 500 μΜ of potentially interfering ions (Cl−, Br−, I−,
PO
4
3 −
,
SO
4
2 −
,
NO
3
−
, CH3COO−), 50 μΜ of heavy metal ions (Mn2+, Ag+, As5+, Se4+, Cr3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Sb+3, Cd2+, Pb+2, Ca2+, K+, Na+, Li+) and 100 μΜ of several amino-acids. The fluorescence quenching caused by the above species alone and in mixtures with (A) 150 μΜ
NO
2
−
and (B) 25 μΜ Fe3+, is displayed in Fig. S10 of Supplementary material. It is clear that the majority of them are not able to quench the fluorescence of CNDs, to a considerable extent, or to affect the quenching caused by the analytes of interest.

Figs. 5–7, illustrate the decrease in fluorescence intensity (through the ratio of F
0 /F, where F
0 and F are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of the quencher, respectively), versus the concentrations of
NO
2
−
,
NO
3
−
and Fe3+, at 25 °C, in the concentration ranges of 0.015–1.11 mM, 72–600 μM and 2.9–176 μΜ, respectively. These plots are a combination of two linear ranges, which are supposed to derive from two fluorophore species, one of them being more accessible to quencher than the other [43]. Moreover, the quenching of fluorescence has a linear relationship with the low-range concentrations of
NO
2
−
(from 0.015 to 1.11 mM),
NO
3
−
(from 0.072 to 0.6 mM) and Fe3+ (from 2.9 to 176 μΜ), with coefficients of determination (R
2 ) of 0.9986, 0.9972 and 0.9968, respectively. The limits of detection (LODs) - calculated as S/N ratio = 3 - were found to be 13.5, 64 and 2.6 μΜ for the detection of
NO
2
−
,
NO
3
−
and Fe3+, respectively.

The applicability of the developed method towards nitrite and nitrate determination using the as-synthesized CNDs was tested in food matrices (flour, Feta cheese, country-style and chicken-originated sausages) after pretreatment. The high-temperature dry-ashing procedure for ferric ion probing precludes the presence of
NO
2
−
,
NO
3
−
in the final solution because these species can undergo thermal decomposition. Likewise, the followed aqueous extraction of
NO
2
−
,
NO
3
−
from the matrices does not allow measurable quantities of iron species to transfer into the solution, as pertinent experiments showed. Our recovery experiments gave satisfactory values at both low (0.11 mM for
NO
2
−
, 0.19 for
NO
3
−
) and high (0.72 mM for
NO
2
−
and 0.5 for
NO
3
−
) spiking concentration levels, ranging between 97% and 101% and 94% and 103% for the
NO
2
−
and 94–104% and 97–99% for the
NO
3
−
detection. The method was compared with a reference method (AOAC) and the results are depicted in Table 1. Although, slightly lower recoveries were noticed with sausages probably due to a subtle matrix effect, the values were fairly acceptable and the detection, overall, displayed satisfactory accuracy.

Furthermore, the method applicability was evaluated for Fe3+ detection in flour, cereals, country-style and chicken sausage samples, by recovery experiments. The recoveries at two spiking concentration levels were satisfactory, ranging from 96% to 107% and 97% to 99% at low (0.044 mM) and high (0.16 mM) Fe3+ concentration, respectively. The results were comparable to those obtained with the Atomic Absorption Spectrometry method, as shown in Table 2. The detection of ferric ion in these matrices was accurate as no matrix effect seemed to occur.

The multi-probe system qualifies as a fluorescent probe for the detection of
NO
2
−
,
NO
3
−
and Fe3+ when the species are simultaneously present in relevant matrices, because the pretreatment followed for the former does not extract the latter and vice versa. The proposed analytical probe rivals other developed analytical methods for
NO
2
−
,
NO
3
−
in relevant matrices, in terms of the recoveries and linear ranges achieved (Table S1 of Supplementary material). It is improved with regard to the time required for the measurements and does not produce toxic products (high-toxicity azo-compounds from the classical Griess reaction). As regards iron in the tested matrices, it is usually determined by atomic absorption and emission techniques, which are more sensitive but require more expensive instrumentation.

The quenching of the emitted fluorescence can occur for various reasons, including static or dynamic quenching, inner-filter effect, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), electron transfer etc. [43]. Fig. 8 demonstrates the normalized fluorescence and absorption spectra, where an overlap is observed between the absorption spectrum of
NO
2
−
and the excitation and emission spectra of CNDs. The energy transfer from the excited state of the donor (i.e. the CNDs) to the acceptor (i.e. the
NO
2
−
) through non-radiative dipole–dipole coupling was the primary potential mechanism (the mathematical equations of FRET are given in Supplementary material). As the distance between the acceptor and donor, r, is lower than 8 nm and the mathematical inequality 0.5R
0 < r < 2R
0 (R
0 , Förster distance) applies, the FRET mechanism is able to describe the quenching of this system. On the contrary, no overlap exists in the spectra of CNDs-Fe3+ system (Fig. S11 of Supplementary material); hence, there is no indication of a FRET mechanism or inner filter effect.

The dependence of the fluorescence quenching on the concentration of the quenchers (
NO
2
−
and Fe3+) exhibited in Figs. 5 and 7, is described by the Stern-Volmer Eq. (1):
(1)
F
0
F
=
1 +
K
SV
[ Q ]
where [Q] is the concentration of the quencher and K SV is the Stern-Volmer constant. The plots were drawn at three different temperatures (25, 35 and 45 °C), in the low linear range of each quencher and are displayed in Fig. S12 (A) and (B) of Supplementary material. In the case of quenching due to
NO
2
−
, there is a directly proportional temperature-dependent relationship with the slopes of the CNDs-
NO
2
−
plots hinting at a dynamic quenching mechanism [44]. This is in agreement with the FRET mechanism mentioned above, which occurs during the donor-acceptor interaction [45,46]. On the other hand, in the case of CNDs-Fe3+ system, the slopes exhibit an inverse temperature dependence. The higher the temperature the more reduced the complex stability; consequently, the induced quenching decreases indicating a static mechanism. Nonetheless, the upward curvature of the Stern-Volmer plot for CNDs-Fe3+ system at high concentrations, which is concave towards the y-axis, entails that the involvement of a combined mechanism (dynamic and static) cannot be ruled out [43]. Finally, EDTA, which is a strong chelating agent failed to restore the fluorescence of the CNDs-Fe3+ system, even at concentrations twenty times higher than that of Fe3+. This leads to the conclusion that a strong complex is formed between Fe3+ and the CNDs through the functional surface groups of the material [3,27].

The binding constant K and the Hill coefficient n which can provide further evidence of the interaction mode of a system can be determined using the following Eq. (2):
(2)
l o g
[
(
F
0
− F
)
/ F
]
= l o g K + n l o g
[ Q ]
where [Q] is the concentration of the quencher. Based on the linear plot of log [(F
0 -F)/F] versus log [Q], the K and n values were obtained from the intercept and the slope, respectively (Figs. S13 and S14 of Supplementary material). The obtained values for the systems CNDs-
NO
2
−
and CNDs-Fe3+ are summarized in Table 3. The number of binding sites “n” shows that the CNDs bind with
NO
2
−
and Fe3+ with approximately one binding site [46].

In this work, we have described an environmentally-friendly synthesis of highly fluorescent N-doped CNDs from two low-cost precursors, namely the Tris and urea. The as-prepared carbon dots demonstrated high photostability under various conditions and were successfully applied to the sensitive detection of nitrite, nitrate and ferric ions. The recoveries (accuracy) were fairly acceptable and the probing systems were free from significant interferences despite the inherent complexity of matrices of the tested food samples. The carbon dots were used for the detection of
NO
2
−
,
NO
3
−
and Fe3+ in food matrices, demonstrating that the as-developed multi-probe system may potentially be implemented for food control purposes.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Many thanks are due to Prof. A. Avgeropoulos for obtaining the TEM image.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2018.07.008.
